source.definition: To administer a vaccine to a person or animal to produce immunity against a specific disease.

explanation.sourceText: The English verb 'vaccinate' derives from the Latin word 'vaccinus', meaning 'of or from cows', referring to the use of cowpox material in the first smallpox vaccinations.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „vaccinate” provine din latinul 'vaccinus', care înseamnă „de la vacă”, referindu-se la utilizarea variolei bovine în primele vaccinări împotriva variolei.

target.definition: A administra un vaccin pentru a crea imunitate împotriva unei boli.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian verb „a vaccina” was borrowed from the French „vacciner”, which comes from the Latin 'vaccinus' (“of cows”), referring to the first smallpox vaccination.
explanation.targetText: Verbul românesc „a vaccina” este împrumutat din francezul „vacciner”, derivat din latinul 'vaccinus' („de la vacă”), referindu-se la prima vaccinare împotriva variolei bovine.

examples.sourceText: I vaccinate my child against measles.
He was vaccinated against the flu yesterday.
examples.targetText: A fost vaccinat ieri împotriva gripei.

examples.sourceText: We have to get vaccinated every year.
examples.targetText: Trebuie să ne vaccinăm în fiecare an.

examples.sourceText: Get vaccinated to protect yourself and others!
examples.targetText: Vaccinează-te pentru a te proteja pe tine și pe ceilalți!

learningTips.text: Remember 'vaccinate' comes from 'vaccine', which relates to cows—think of the first vaccines coming from cowpox.
The Romanian verb 'a vaccina' sounds like 'vaccinate' in English, making it easy to recall their connection.
Notice the '-ina' ending in 'a vaccina'—many Romanian first conjugation verbs share this, like 'a lumina' (to illuminate).
Imagine a vacuum sucking up germs—'vaccinate' protects you by removing threats, just like a vacuum cleans.
